title: What risk actually means
subtitle: Risk is not a feeling. It is a measurable property of outcomes. Learning to define it precisely is the first step to managing it.

body.content: The word risk is used in finance to mean at least four different things: the probability of loss, the magnitude of loss, the volatility of returns, and the uncertainty of outcomes. These are related but distinct. Conflating them leads to poor decisions.
Risk as probability vs. risk as magnitude

A 90% chance of losing $1 is a high-probability risk. A 1% chance of losing everything is a low-probability, high-magnitude risk. Treating them identically — as most intuitive risk assessments do — is a mistake. Expected value (probability multiplied by magnitude) is a more useful starting point than gut feeling.

body.label: Key concept
body.content: In financial markets, risk is most commonly measured as standard deviation of returns — how widely actual outcomes are dispersed around the average. This is volatility. High volatility means outcomes are unpredictable. It does not necessarily mean you will lose money.

The practical implication: a highly volatile investment is not automatically more dangerous than a stable one. What matters is whether the volatility occurs within a time horizon you can sustain. Volatility over a 30-year investment window is almost irrelevant. Volatility in a position you need to exit in 18 months can be catastrophic.
Risk is not a single number. It is probability, magnitude, and time horizon in combination. Define all three before you evaluate any investment — and before you decide how you feel about it.
